data.title: Federal Reserve Chair nominee Warsh: Technology-driven deflation is a major risk
data.description:
Author: Anthony J. Pompliano, Founder and CEO of Professional Capital Management; Translated by Shaw Jinse Finance

Last April, the US President took to the podium in the White House Rose Garden, triggering a violent upheaval in the financial markets. He announced a comprehensive tariff increase on all US imports, causing the stock market to plummet, academics to panic, and the internet to be filled with various predictions of an impending Great Depression.

Of course, this intense reaction was purely unwarranted. The stock market subsequently rebounded, repeatedly hitting new historical highs. A host of "experts" made inaccurate predictions, while those investors who ignored the noise and made rational investments reaped substantial profits.

However, one viewpoint in the mainstream analysis at the time seemed utterly absurd to me: various "experts" kept claiming that tariff policies would fuel hyperinflation. In contrast, I repeatedly emphasized in April 2025 that deflation was a far more serious threat than inflation. In a tweet on April 10, 2025, I wrote: "All the herd-following financial professionals are telling you that tariffs will drive up inflation, but I want to make it clear that the greater risk is actually deflation." To this day, I still maintain this judgment. You don't need to believe me, and you don't need to listen to me elaborate further. Kevin Warsh, the next chairman of the Federal Reserve, recently stated clearly during an interview with CNBC that he believes technological advancements will lead to a structural decline in prices, and that deflation is a serious risk that the Federal Reserve must prepare in advance. Artificial intelligence will lower the cost of all goods and services. We may be ushering in the beginning of a new round of productivity boom, and the major trend of structural price declines may have just begun. These remarks were made publicly by the soon-to-be head of the Federal Reserve on a national television program. If you still cannot clearly predict that the Federal Reserve will continue to cut interest rates in the coming months or even years, then probably no information can convince you. Some might claim that Warsh is merely a puppet of Trump, well aware that he must cut interest rates to avoid conflict with the president. Others argue that short-term increases in oil prices and long-term increases in commodity prices will push up inflation, thus limiting the Federal Reserve's room for significant interest rate cuts. I have no way of knowing Warsh's political stance, nor how he will work with the president. Only time will tell if these doubts are true. However, regarding the claim of rising inflation, in my view, deflation still poses a far greater threat to the US economy than inflation—this is beyond doubt. Oil and gas prices are indeed rising, as are commodity prices. But the US economy today is more reliant on the technology industry than ever before. Crucially, technology is significantly depressing the prices of various goods and services in the US. The structural decline in prices mentioned by Walsh has long been misunderstood by the public. As Jeff Booth once pointed out, "The natural state of a free market is deflation; prices will fall back to the marginal cost of production." I agree with this view. If this theory holds true, with the accelerated popularization of artificial intelligence and robotics, a continued decline in prices will become inevitable. Those who hold a rational opposing view will immediately point out that the US government's continuous money printing, the ever-growing national debt, and unrestrained fiscal spending have almost always triggered inflation historically. I do not deny that past experience has indeed confirmed this pattern. However, the current situation is unique in that a powerful deflationary force is dominating the US economy. Tariff policies, immigration deportations, artificial intelligence, and automation technologies—multiple factors combined—have formed a highly impactful deflationary force. As Elon Musk said, even if the US prints unlimited amounts of money, it will be difficult to offset this strong deflationary momentum. This argument may sound absurd and even seem jarring. But the more I delve into the data, the more convinced I become that deflation is the greater risk at present. The first major test for this view was last year's tariff policy. At that time, the market generally predicted a surge in inflation; however, deflationary forces directly suppressed inflation, and the price spike never materialized. The second crucial test will arrive in the next two to three months. Most investors expect inflation to rebound sharply, with the Consumer Price Index (CPI) rising by more than 5% year-on-year; however, real-time inflation indicators such as Truflation suggest that the CPI will likely remain moderate. If the CPI does not surge, astute market participants will see that deflationary forces are profoundly impacting the overall economy. Finally, and ultimately, the test: will there be a substantial decline in consumer prices? A halt to price increases is far from enough; it only alleviates cost pressures and cannot create a true deflationary dividend or reduce the burden of living. Price stabilization merely means that the cost of living has stopped worsening. However, once the prices of consumer goods for ordinary people fall across the board, the US economy may enter a golden age of development, entering an ideal environment of high growth and low inflation. This is precisely the economic scenario that central bank governors around the world dream of. All Americans should expect that artificial intelligence companies will bring a strong deflationary effect, boosting GDP while lowering the cost of living. Once this vision is realized, all politicians, central bank officials, and regulatory agencies will be seen as heroes of the era. Who claims credit for this economic victory is not important; the American people simply want lower prices to embrace economic prosperity. May we eventually achieve our goal.

data.title: Aave suspends rsETH reserve operations on multiple networks; US-Iran negotiations may be at a standstill.
data.description:
Headline

▌Aave Suspends rsETH Reserve Operations on Ethereum, Arbitrum, and Other Networks to Prevent Expanding Liquidation Risk

On April 23, Aave released the latest developments regarding the rsETH security incident on the X platform. It disclosed that it has suspended rsETH reserve operations on the Ethereum mainnet and networks such as Arbitrum, Base, Mantle, and Linea to prevent excess rsETH from being withdrawn, thereby pushing the position close to the 95% liquidation threshold. This measure aims to preserve as much capital as possible and reduce systemic risk during the asset recovery process. Aave stated that subsequent progress and disposal plans will continue to be disclosed to the community.

On April 23, Saudi Arabia's Al Arabiya television quoted a Pakistani diplomat as saying that negotiations between the United States and Iran were "at an impasse" and progressing "very slowly." The diplomat said that the United States remains committed to maintaining its naval blockade against Iran. Iran has informed Pakistan that the US blockade is an obstacle to its participation in the negotiations.

▌Iranian Source: Breakthrough Possible in Iran-US Negotiations

April 23 - An Iranian diplomatic source told RIA Novosti on the 23rd that preparations for negotiations between Iran and the United States in Pakistan may achieve a breakthrough "tonight or tomorrow." (Xinhua)

The number of initial jobless claims in the United States last week was 214,000, exceeding the estimated 210,000 and the previous week's 207,000.

▌US Aircraft Carrier Heads to the Middle East; Trump Issues "Sinking Order"; Iran Reveals Seven Countermeasure Plans

On April 24, according to CCTV, as the standoff between the US and Iran escalated in the Strait of Hormuz on the 23rd local time, the US nuclear-powered aircraft carrier USS George H.W. Bush was heading towards the Middle East. Currently, although the ceasefire between the US and Iran has been maintained, it remains unclear when a new round of negotiations will begin. Pakistani diplomats stated that US-Iran negotiations have "reached a stalemate," while US President Trump stated that "Iran has no time" and ordered the US military to sink any vessel that has laid mines in the Strait of Hormuz. The Israeli Defense Minister stated that he is prepared to resume hostilities and is awaiting the US's "green light." Meanwhile, given its loss of trust in the US's sincerity in negotiations, Iran has developed targeted countermeasure plans against "possible" military actions by the US and its allies.

▌The probability of the Fed keeping interest rates unchanged in April is 99%

On April 24th, according to CME's "FedWatch": the probability of the Fed raising interest rates by 25 basis points in April is 1%, and the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 99%. The probability of the Fed cumulatively cutting interest rates by 25 basis points by June is 2.6%, the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 96.4%, and the probability of cumulatively raising interest rates by 25 basis points is 1%.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Bitcoin still be called "digital gold" under geopolitical crises?

Every time there is a geopolitical crisis, gold prices rise, and Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been confirmed by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves.

For investors, Bitcoin exhibits an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but doesn't rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an overabundance of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," capable of functioning when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: CoinEx Founder: My View of the Crypto Endgame
data.description:
Author: Yang Haipo, Founder of CoinEx

This article was written on April 10th

I. Bitcoin is a Pure Consensus Asset

Bitcoin has no productive value, no consumer value, and no real monetary function. Historically, there are very few precedents for pure consensus assets to survive for a long time.

The analogy with gold is invalid. Gold has nearly half of the physical consumer demand (jewelry, industry), has served as a form of currency between sovereign states for thousands of years, and has zero maintenance costs—a gold bar can be kept in a safe for a hundred years without anyone maintaining it. Bitcoin lacks all three of these qualities. In the era of fiat currency, between sovereign states, gold remains the most rigid form of currency—it is the only substance discovered by mankind that can store value without relying on any third party. Bitcoin relies on the power grid, the internet, miners, and exchanges; if any one of these links is cut off, it will be paralyzed.

Bitcoin once possessed some genuine monetary function—dark web transactions, cross-border transfers, and micropayments. This could have served as its value anchor. However, in the scaling debate, the Core faction prevailed, choosing the small-block route and voluntarily abandoning payment functionality. At that moment, Bitcoin degenerated from a "flawed currency" into a "pure consensus speculative asset." Subsequent institutional entry and ETFs merely prolonged the life of an asset that had lost its functionality. Security budgets are a self-destructive mechanism. With block rewards continuously halving and approaching zero, network security ultimately relies entirely on transaction fees. However, the inherent contradiction between the "hold and hold" value narrative and the security model that "requires transaction fees" remains unresolved. Bitcoin's price successfully masked all of this. Price is the strongest market signal, and most people are powerless to resist it. Price created path dependence—because it rose, there were ETFs, institutional holdings, and the "too big to fail" narrative. However, the foundation of this chain is consensus. Once the price trend reverses, the same chain will accelerate in the opposite direction. Cryptocurrencies won't completely go to zero—free trading, censorship resistance, and permissionless transfers still have some value, providing a floor far below current prices. But a significant collapse from the current trillion-dollar market capitalization is inevitable. II. The First Principles of a Negative-Sum System Understanding this system requires only one equation: Net Inflow = Historical Consumption + Margin Balance Once money enters the system, it only goes to two places: either it's consumed (electricity bills, wages, rent, legal fees, personal extravagance), permanently leaving the system; or it remains in the system (stablecoins + fiat currency balance, i.e., margin). There is no third place it can go. All the numerical deductions in this article are essentially filling the variables in this equation from different paths. The entire system has an annual fixed cost of approximately $350-500 billion, potentially even higher during boom periods: mining costs around $100-150 billion (electricity, mining machines, and facilities), exchange operations around $150-250 billion (human resources, cloud services, compliance, and marketing), project operations several billion, and other related expenses several hundred million. This scale can also be verified by working backwards from the number of people employed in the industry. The global crypto industry has approximately 1.6 million broadly defined practitioners (as of 2025), but a large portion of these are part-time participants, KOLs, professional traders, and other non-full-time positions. The core practitioners who truly make a living from the crypto market number approximately 100,000-200,000: 50,000-100,000 in exchanges, 30,000-50,000 in project teams, 20,000-50,000 in mining, and 10,000-30,000 in service providers (law firms, compliance, media, VCs, market makers, etc.). Based on an estimated annual cost of $200,000 per person (including salaries, office space, infrastructure, compliance, marketing, etc., all diluted), human resources and related costs are approximately 20-40 billion RMB per year. Adding the 10-15 billion RMB for mining, the total expenditure is 30-55 billion RMB, consistent with the estimated annual expenditure of 35-50 billion RMB. The system's actual external revenue is extremely weak. Stablecoin payments, cross-border transfers, and some on-chain settlements do generate some external demand, but relative to the total market capitalization and industry costs of the entire crypto market, this revenue is far from sufficient to support the current scale. Transaction fees are essentially an internal cycle—the money users pay to exchanges comes from their own principal, not from external clients. Mining-generated coins also circulate internally. The only source that can significantly replenish funds is the inflow of new investors. Once the inflow falls below the annual expenditure of tens of billions of RMB, the system is experiencing a net outflow. This is fundamentally different from traditional finance. The underlying companies in the stock market are creating real profits—Apple earns over $90 billion a year, and the entire S&P 500 generates trillions of dollars in annual profits. Transaction costs are negligible compared to the profit pool, so the stock market's long-term growth isn't driven by new capital inflows, but by the continuous profits made by the underlying companies. The crypto industry, however, doesn't profit from the outside world; it's a purely negative-sum game. The structure of the crypto industry is highly similar to the casino industry: exchanges are the casinos, miners are the infrastructure maintainers, projects are the different gambling tables, and a whole host of parasitic industries have sprung up around them—media, KOLs, summits, investment institutions, law firms, compliance companies. Gambling is the traffic driver; the real consumption occurs throughout the entire industry chain. But the key difference is: casino gamblers know they are gambling, while the crypto industry packages casinos as a "revolution," "the future financial infrastructure," and "digital gold," making gamblers believe they are investing, even participating in a great cause. The real winners are the harvesters on the periphery of the system: power companies, chip manufacturers, cloud service providers, landlords, and luxury goods retailers. This rigid cost is like gravity, the fundamental reason why bear markets inevitably exist. Bull markets are merely an illusion where the inflow of new funds temporarily exceeds the rate of consumption. III. Historical Dissipation of Trillions of Dollars The industry's cumulative operating costs are approximately $500 billion: mining has accumulated approximately $150 billion, exchanges have accumulated approximately $200 billion, and other investments and projects have accumulated approximately $150-200 billion (VC alone accounts for $100-120 billion, plus direct financing such as ICOs and the project's own operating costs). The $200 billion for exchanges can be broken down: Coinbase alone, from 2021 to 2025, accumulated operating expenses were approximately $24 billion (including revenue costs), plus early investments from 2012 to 2020, bringing the total historical cumulative costs to $25-27 billion. Binance doesn't release public financial reports, but based on its staff size and global compliance spending, the cumulative costs are estimated to be on the same scale. These two alone have consumed approximately $50-60 billion. Adding to this the hundreds of exchanges that have historically been involved—Huobi, OKX, FTX, Bitfinex, Kraken, Bybit, Kucoin, Gate, Mt. Gox, etc.—200 billion is a conservative estimate. The $150 billion from mining isn't just about Bitcoin. Bitcoin's cumulative electricity costs plus mining equipment over fifteen years amounted to approximately $100 billion, all of which became electricity costs and scrap metal. Ethereum's cumulative revenue for miners over its seven-year Proof-of-Work (PoW) cycle was approximately $30-40 billion, with corresponding hardware and electricity investments on the same scale. On the day of the PoS transition in 2022, approximately $19 billion in hardware assets vanished overnight. FIL is even more extreme—a triple trap of hardware, staking, and installment debt. Before the mainnet launch in the Chinese market alone, mining equipment sales exceeded 30 billion RMB, and FIL plummeted from a peak of $237 to less than $1. Miners of other Proof-of-Work (PoW) cryptocurrencies like LTC and DOGE face a similar situation, with their actual holding costs far exceeding the current market price. However, the 500 billion figure only represents operational costs at the enterprise level. The largest underestimated component is the spillover of personal consumption by participants. Of the hundreds of millions of crypto users globally, the vast majority entered the market during bull runs, and a significant proportion did indeed make real money – after selling their tokens, they lavishly spent on luxury cars, mansions, watches, nightclubs, casinos, and other luxury goods. During events like Token2049, entire cities become crypto-industry consumption extravaganzas. There's also a more hidden consumption channel: the income of many full-time KOLs and professional traders comes entirely from the crypto market. All their living expenses – rent, food, travel, luxury goods – are essentially operating costs of the system, just not recorded on any company's books. These "profits," seemingly extracted by winners, are mostly never retained as personal capital; instead, they flow directly back to the system's consumption layer – like a gambler winning money in Macau and then spending it right next to the casino; the money never truly leaves the ecosystem. The total scale of this spillover of personal consumption may be no less than that of corporate operating costs, but it will never be statistically accounted for. Adding to this theft and confiscation totaling 30-50 billion yuan, the total deadweight of the industry has reached the trillion-dollar level, and continues to grow at a rate of tens of billions of dollars annually. All data also suffers from systemic underestimation: the costs of numerous small and medium-sized exchanges and defunct projects are not statistically analyzed, and the consumption of peripheral industries spurred by the bull market is not included in any statistical scope. IV. True Circulating Market Capitalization and System Leverage Ratio The current total market capitalization of cryptocurrencies is approximately $2.5 trillion, but it's necessary to gradually separate the non-trading portions: stablecoins and RWA, totaling approximately $340 billion, are not crypto assets; approximately 3-4 million BTC have been permanently lost (including approximately 1 million belonging to Satoshi Nakamoto), corresponding to a market capitalization of approximately $250 billion; altcoins have a total nominal market capitalization of approximately $500 billion, but most of the supply is concentrated in the hands of founding teams and VCs (approximately 45% of XRP is held by Ripple, and approximately 60-80% of BNB is held by CZ and Binance). Conservatively, this should be halved. After this separation, the true circulating market capitalization of the crypto market is approximately $1.6 trillion, of which BTC accounts for 72%—the fate of the entire market almost entirely depends on the price of a single coin. Regarding margin requirements, the combined market capitalization of USDT and USDC is approximately $250 billion. However, other stablecoins (USDe, DAI, etc.) are largely nested funds minted using USDT/USDC as underlying collateral and should not be counted twice. A significant portion of this $250 billion is used for non-crypto speculative purposes such as cross-border payments, remittances, and corporate settlements. Excluding these, the stablecoins truly serving as margin for the crypto market are approximately $150-180 billion. Adding the two to three hundred billion in fiat currency balances held by fiat exchanges, the total margin is approximately $200 billion. The $1.6 trillion real circulating market capitalization corresponds to approximately $200 billion in margin, resulting in an effective leverage ratio of approximately 8 times. The system is far more fragile than it appears—if only 5% of holders simultaneously want to cash out for fiat currency, liquidity will dry up, and prices will collapse. Hundreds of billions of dollars in operational costs are continuously draining margin annually. Without a continuous inflow of new funds, the margin pool will continue to shrink. The essence of a bull market is the growth of stablecoin balances—new funds enter to mint stablecoins, the margin pool expands, and the leverage effect amplifies into a surge in market capitalization. In a bear market, the opposite occurs: stablecoins are redeemed, margins shrink, and market capitalization collapses rapidly. This structure continues to deteriorate—the margin ratio continues to decline, while the leverage ratio continues to rise, until it can no longer sustain itself. V. ETFs and DAT: The Last Infusion of Funds In 2024-2025, the crypto market experienced a seemingly strong bull market: BTC rose from approximately $40,000 to over $120,000. The mainstream narrative attributes this to "institutional endorsement" and "mainstream adoption." However, if we ignore price and only look at fund flows, the conclusion is completely opposite. The incremental funds in this bull market almost entirely came from two channels: ETFs/ETPs saw a cumulative net inflow of approximately $100-110 billion; and DAT companies (digital asset treasury companies, represented by Strategy/MicroStrategy) invested approximately $90-100 billion. Together, this amounted to approximately $200 billion in real fiat currency inflows. Putting this figure back into the system's capital flow model: at the end of 2022, the margin pool of the crypto system was approximately $120 billion. Between 2023 and 2025, the industry's rigid consumption is estimated at approximately $100-150 billion. Without the $200 billion inflow from ETFs and DAT, the margin pool would have been depleted to near zero by around 2025. ETFs and DAT are not merely "icing on the cake" institutional endorsements, but the sole reason preventing system collapse. This $200 billion is a transfusion, not a source of new capital. The current margin pool is approximately 200 billion, which is exactly equal to the cumulative net inflow of ETFs and DAT—meaning that excluding these two channels, the net increase in funds within the crypto ecosystem is zero or even negative. Hundreds of millions of users, hundreds of exchanges, thousands of projects, and the entire DeFi ecosystem—all have contributed zero net over three years. The internal economy is already a closed zero-sum cycle. This conclusion can be cross-validated: ETFs and DAT inflows totaled 200 billion, and the net increase in the margin pool was approximately 80 billion (from 120 billion to 200 billion). The 120 billion that disappeared in between falls precisely within the estimated range of industry consumption over three years. Two completely independent paths—one calculating annual consumption from the bottom up based on the industry cost structure, and the other calculating net losses backward from fund inflows and outflows—converge to the same number. This also explains an unprecedented phenomenon: BTC hits new highs, but ETH and altcoins do not. Previously, during each bull market, BTC and altcoins moved in tandem because new entrants would spread funds from BTC to the entire ecosystem. This time, funds went directly to BTC through ETFs, without any diffusion. ETFs brought in funds, but not users—someone who bought IBIT clicked "buy" on a brokerage app; they didn't download Binance, see the altcoin list, join Telegram groups, or participate in airdrops. ETFs didn't cut off the flow of funds, but the flow of people. Money was channeled directly to the BTC table, but the casino lobby was empty. And these two channels of capital infusion are closing. In reality, only Strategy is buying DAT anymore; other DAT companies have only purchased about 1,000 BTC in the past 30 days, a 99% decrease from the peak. Strategy itself is a leverage bomb—buying 767,000 BTC at a cost of 58 billion, an average price of about $75,000, and starting to lose money when BTC fell below $70,000. From the largest buyer to the largest potential seller, it's all happened in an instant. ETFs are a double-edged sword – during the BTC pullback in early 2026, there were several consecutive weeks of net outflows. ETF holders are not believers, but asset allocation investors who will cut their losses when they reach a certain level. ETF redemptions directly translate into spot selling pressure: redemption → authorized participants sell BTC → price drops → more redemptions. This reflexive cycle is amplified dramatically in the less liquid crypto market. VI. The Buyer List is Exhausted Historically, every bear market in crypto seems like a near-death experience, but it always manages to survive. This isn't because the system itself has the ability to generate revenue, but because each time it happens to find the next group of investors bringing net inflows of funds: the 2014 bear market was saved by the awakening of retail investors in 2017; the 2018 bear market was saved by the DeFi, NFT, MEME, and other concept craze and the large-scale influx of retail investors in 2020-2021; and the 2022 bear market was saved by ETFs in 2024. Every time it "didn't crash," it was attributed to "crypto resilience" and "Bitcoin won't die," further reinforcing this belief. But the truth isn't resilience, it's luck—each time, new investors were found just before the margin was about to run out. This list of investors is running out. The number of new investors that can be brought in with each round is decreasing, and the mainstream, accessible investor base has been largely covered. There are already hundreds of millions of crypto users globally, major economies have regulatory frameworks in place, and mainstream financial institutions have either stated their support or entered the market. Those who need to know about Bitcoin already know, and most of those who want to buy have already bought; the pool of new funds is shrinking rapidly. The 2028 halving might bring another surge, but where will the marginal buyers come from? Retail investors have already been exploited several times, institutions have entered, and ETFs have been launched. Previously, each bull market relied on opening up a new investor base, but now there are few large groups waiting to be developed. Some hope that central banks will include Bitcoin in their reserves, but this is unrealistic—central bank reserve assets must possess high liquidity, low volatility, and sovereign credit backing, all of which Bitcoin fails to meet. More fundamentally, the function of central banks is to maintain the credit of fiat currency; purchasing a cash-flow-free asset that competes with fiat currency and is based on the narrative of replacing it is, logically, self-contradictory. The so-called "US strategic BTC reserve" is essentially a collection and naming of existing confiscated assets, not the entry of new money. Political statements by individual countries do not equate to actual asset allocation behavior. Furthermore, the amplifying effect of marginal pricing means that changes in capital flows will be dramatically amplified in market capitalization. Over the past three years, a real inflow of 200 billion yuan has leveraged a market capitalization expansion of approximately 2.5 trillion yuan—an amplification factor exceeding 10 times. The reverse is also true: if capital begins to flow out, the speed and magnitude of market capitalization evaporation will be more than ten times the outflow amount. Moreover, the amplification effect of outflows is more intense than that of inflows—inflows are dispersed, proactive behaviors lasting several months, while outflows may be concentrated, panic-driven, and passive behaviors completed within a few weeks. VII. Timeline Adding in the spillover of personal consumption, the system's actual annual consumption is likely 60-80 billion. This figure can be verified by working backward from the capital flow: over the past three years, ETF+DAT inflows totaled 200 billion, plus positive inflows from retail investors and other channels, bringing the total inflow to approximately 250-300 billion. However, margin deposits only increased by 80 billion, meaning the consumed 170-220 billion corresponds to an annual average of 60-70 billion. Of this, 35-50 billion is traceable operational consumption at the enterprise level, and the remaining several tens of billions is the personal consumption spillover described in Chapter 3—this portion will not appear in any company's financial statements, but it has indeed permanently left the system. Based on actual consumption of 60-80 billion per year, 200 billion in margin deposits would only last 2.5-3 years. This is an optimistic scenario assuming no net selling. Reality will be much harsher—bear markets are always accompanied by net selling. Referencing 2022, stablecoins lost 65 billion in less than a year. If a round of panic redemptions is added, it's equivalent to more than half a year's worth of consumption being drained away in just a few months. More importantly, industry spending has inertia: revenue reflects the market in real time; a decrease in trading volume leads to a drop in fees that month. However, spending has rigidity and lag—employee contracts cannot be terminated on the same day, office leases are signed annually, mining farm power contracts have lock-up periods, and compliance license maintenance fees are not reduced due to a poor market. In the early stages of a market downturn, there will be a scissor-like situation where revenue shrinks rapidly but spending remains high, with net consumption occurring even faster than in a bull market. The system doesn't need to reach zero margin to collapse. If margin drops from 200 billion to 100 billion, the market capitalization might already be plummeting from over 2 trillion to 500-600 billion. At that stage, exchanges will shut down en masse, project teams will abscond, miners will shut down, and the entire industry will enter a death spiral—consumption will indeed decrease as the industry contracts, but the rate of decrease will be slower than the rate of system shrinkage. It's like a person losing weight while losing blood; metabolism does slow down, but the rate of decrease can't keep up with the rate of blood loss. The 2028 halving will be the true litmus test. If BTC fails to reach a new high after the halving, the belief that "it always comes back" will be shattered for the first time. Previously, during bear markets, the discussion was "where is the bottom?"; this time, the discussion will become "is there still a bottom?" At that moment, panic selling will no longer be about cutting losses, but about escaping a sinking system. The direction is certain, but the pace is uncertain.

VIII. If the Crypto Industry Were a Company

Imagine the entire crypto industry as a company, and open its financial statements:

Profit and Loss Statement—This company has almost no revenue. Its main business is operating an internal trading market where users buy and sell digital tokens created by the company, and the company takes a commission from the transactions. It looks like "revenue," but the source is entirely the principal invested by users; there are no external customers paying for it. Real external revenue (such as stablecoin cross-border payments) may only be a few hundred million US dollars per year, covering less than 1% of operating costs. At the same time, annual operating costs are as high as 60-80 billion US dollars.

Balance Sheet—The cumulative amount of money burned over the past trillions of dollars. There is only about 200 billion US dollars left in cash.

However, the company's "market capitalization" is listed as over two trillion—this figure is an illusion created by leveraging 200 billion in cash through marginal pricing at approximately 8 times leverage, and cannot be cashed out by all shareholders simultaneously. If everyone wanted to sell at the same time, the total amount they could get back was only that 200 billion. Financing History—The company has never been profitable and relies entirely on external financing to survive. In 2017, it raised a Series A round (from retail investors); in 2020-2021, it raised a Series B round (driven by a nationwide influx of investors due to the concept frenzy); and in 2024-2025, it raised a Series C round (from ETFs and DAT). Each round was larger because the rate of cash burn also accelerated. The latest round raised approximately 200 billion, sustaining operations for three years, just enough to replenish the cash on hand to 200 billion. However, the investor list is exhausted, and a Series D round is not possible. Fatal Feature—The company's users and shareholders almost completely overlap. Those who buy cryptocurrency are both "users" (using the trading market) and "shareholders" (holding tokens and sharing in the appreciation). This means that user churn = shareholder redemption = decreased revenue = decreased market value; all four events occur simultaneously without any buffer.

Nine, Most People Overestimate Their Winning Probability

In a negative-sum system, the overall return for participants is inevitably negative—the money consumed by the system won't magically return. However, the distribution at the individual level is not uniform. A minority do indeed make real money and leave the market, but they represent a very small percentage of all participants.

The predicament faced by most people is structural. Breaking it down by role: Novices tend to enter at the end of a bull market and exit at the bottom of a bear market, naturally placing themselves on the opposite side of probability; however, they lose early, and the damage is relatively controllable. Professional gamblers win and then lose it all back, cycling through several times until their capital is zero. The problem isn't skill but the gambler's psychology.

Believers hold onto Bitcoin's gains because of their faith, but this same faith also prevents them from ever selling—faith is the reason for their success, and it could also be the reason for their downfall. Exchange and project leaders may appear to be market manipulators, but most simply accumulate more coins and invest in new projects, their profits never truly leaving the system. Some have even established family offices claiming to be transitioning to investing in US stocks, but their entire holdings are in IBIT—moving Bitcoin from cold wallets to ETFs, changing the guise, but with the same underlying exposure. The dilemma of believers is particularly worth exploring. The crypto industry presents a subtle paradox: making big money on Bitcoin requires early purchase and long-term holding, which is only achievable by believers—those who don't believe can't hold on. But after making money, the price increase is attributed by the brain to "my faith is right," creating a positive feedback loop that locks in that faith. The screening mechanism ensures that only believers can make money, and the process of making money ensures that believers never leave the market. Breaking this cycle requires an extremely rare ability—to logically refute one's own beliefs even when those beliefs are continuously validated by the market. This is almost counterintuitive. BSV is the most direct counter-example. The BSV community's level of faith is probably the highest among all crypto projects, with core holders far more steadfast than most mainstream cryptocurrencies, yet this doesn't affect the continued decline in BSV's price. The reason is simple—no new funds are coming in. Asset prices are marginal pricing, not a vote of faith. Ten thousand die-hard holders holding on is less valuable than a new user entering the market with real money. Faith is not the bottom support; new funds are. Those who truly leave with large sums of money are extremely rare. Their common characteristic isn't good foresight, but knowing when to leave the table. They generate revenue by building real businesses rather than purely gambling on the market, transferring the vast majority of their assets to the real economy during industry booms, completely transcending the crypto narrative in their understanding. Among hundreds of millions of participants, those who achieve all of the above are probably less than one in a thousand. The vast majority of believers will ride this rollercoaster ride to the end—they're there when they go up, and they're there when they come down. While the price is still high, everything looks respectable, but as soon as the price trend reverses, their true colors will be revealed one by one. Ten, a pyramid scheme more efficient than a pyramid scheme. If strictly defined by the flow of funds: the source of income for existing participants is not the productive return of the underlying assets, but the new funds injected by subsequent participants—this is the capital structure of a pyramid scheme. The crypto industry perfectly fits this definition. The difference from classic pyramid schemes is only in form. Traditional pyramid schemes have explicit hierarchies and referral commission mechanisms, while crypto attracts new members through narratives—ICOs talk about technological revolution, DeFi talks about financial democratization, NFTs talk about digital ownership, and Meme coins don't even pretend; they directly talk about gambling. Each round of narratives may have a different veneer, but the underlying capital flow logic remains exactly the same: early entrants exit using money from later entrants, with no real value increment generated at any stage. However, what makes crypto more terrifying than traditional pyramid schemes is that traditional pyramid schemes are scams, and scams have a natural ceiling on scale. Scams need to maintain the lie; once someone exposes it, someone reports it to the police, or someone discovers the product doesn't exist, the chain of propagation breaks. Most people have a basic immunity to scams, so the reach of traditional pyramid schemes is always limited. Crypto doesn't scam. BTC does exist, the blockchain does operate, the coins you buy are indeed in your wallet, and the transactions are indeed traceable on the chain. Everything is real. This bypasses humanity's most basic defense mechanism against fraud—the question "Is this fake?"—which fails in the face of crypto because it is genuinely not fake. But "real existence" and "valuable" are two different things. A technically functional system does not mean that the assets it supports have intrinsic value. The strange thing about crypto is that it substitutes technological authenticity for economic value, and most people can't distinguish between the two. People see the chain running, the coin rising, the exchange operating, and ETFs listed, so they naturally assume it's a "real asset class." No one feels cheated because, on the surface, no one is actually cheating them. This is why crypto reaches a far larger population than any traditional pyramid scheme—it doesn't trigger any fraud alarms. A traditional pyramid scheme is considered a major case if it defrauds hundreds of thousands of people; crypto easily involves hundreds of millions, and most of them still don't consider themselves victims. The only thing that partially exempts this judgment is a very thin layer of instrumental use—cross-border settlements, censorship-resistant transfers, and stablecoin payments. This part is real, but it accounts for a very small percentage of the industry's overall cash flow, and it doesn't require BTC to be worth hundreds of thousands of dollars. USDT can be used for cross-border settlements without BTC having a trillion-dollar market capitalization. This instrumental use is real, but it can't support an "industry," let alone a trillion-dollar market capitalization. Conclusion The above deductions do not rely on any specific figures. Even if each figure has a 50% margin of error, the conclusion remains unchanged. Precise data is unavailable in this industry—it's opaque, there are no statistics on defunct projects, no records of over-the-counter transactions, and no explanation for individual extravagance. All figures are orders of magnitude judgments, not precise calculations. But that's enough. Investment decisions and strategic judgments never need to be precise to the decimal point; they only need to be of the right direction and have a credible magnitude. From a macro perspective, the entire logical chain is clear: a high-consumption system with no external revenue has historically dissipated over a trillion dollars, and currently only about 200 billion in real margin is left, leveraging 8 times to support a circulating market capitalization of 1.6 trillion. The last large-scale external injection (ETF + DAT) has been used up, the internal economic net growth is zero, and the buyer list is exhausted. The system is still bleeding billions every year, while the injection channels are closing. The industry won't disappear, but it will shrink drastically. The needs for censorship-resistant transfers and free trade are real and will persist for a long time, just on a much smaller scale than now. Eventually, the industry will shrink to a size commensurate with the actual inflow of funds—reaching equilibrium when the new inflow just covers the system's operational costs. The market capitalization at that equilibrium might be only a fraction of its current value. However, there's an even more extreme possibility: Bitcoin might not even reach equilibrium. A sustained price drop would lead to a massive shutdown of miners, a sharp decline in network hashrate, and a flood of cheaply sold mining machines—drastically reducing the cost of a 51% attack. A successful attack would permanently destroy Bitcoin's security reputation, further collapsing the price, causing further loss of hashrate, and further lowering the cost of attacks. This is a spiral towards zero. In this scenario, cryptocurrency as a speculative market dies completely, with the only survivor being stablecoins as payment channels—but that's no longer the "crypto industry," it's just a corner of financial infrastructure. This is the most expensive social experiment in human history regarding whether consensus can replace values. The answer is already clear, but most participants are unwilling to admit it. The inlet is closing, but the outlet never stops. The direction is certain.

Headline

US Military Admits to Running Bitcoin Nodes

On April 23, General Samuel Paparo, Commander of the US Indo-Pacific Command, admitted at a hearing of the US House Armed Services Committee that the US military is running a Bitcoin node, but not for mining. Instead, it is using it to monitor networks and conduct a series of operational tests to ensure the security of networks using the Bitcoin protocol. Paparo stated that the US military is using Bitcoin nodes to protect network security. The US military's interest in Bitcoin lies in its potential as a cryptographic tool, blockchain, and reusable proof-of-work mechanism, serving as an auxiliary means of ensuring network security and projecting power. He also stated that Bitcoin has a direct impact on national security and supports any measures that help maintain the dollar's global dominance.

Headline

US Military Admits to Running Bitcoin Nodes

AI Hot Topics 1. Anthropic's Secondary Market Valuation Rises to $1 Trillion, Surpassing OpenAI On April 23, Anthropic's valuation on private equity trading platforms such as Forge Global rose to approximately $1 trillion, surpassing OpenAI's $880 billion. It is reported that the valuation of this artificial intelligence startup has rapidly increased due to buyers scrambling to acquire Anthropic's dwindling secondary market stock. 2. OpenAI Plans to Invest Up to $1.5 Billion in Private Equity Joint Venture DeployCo On April 23, it was announced that OpenAI is forming a joint venture, DeployCo, with several private equity firms. Registered in Delaware, OpenAI will hold a majority stake and super voting rights. OpenAI initially invested $500 million in equity, with the option to raise an additional $1 billion; TPG, Bain Capital, Advent International, Brookfield, and Goanna Capital invested an additional $4 billion. DeployCo is expected to complete its funding round in early May, valuing the company at $10 billion. DeployCo is currently managed by former OpenAI COO Brad Lightcap and has begun recruiting its own staff, while OpenAI is also seconding personnel. Lightcap previously spearheaded the recruitment of dozens of "forward deployed engineers," embedding them within client companies to help implement technology—a model pioneered by Palantir. DeployCo's business model involves charging portfolio companies of PE firms to help them embed AI into their business processes. PE investors are locked in for five years, and OpenAI guarantees at least a 17.5% annual return; sources say, "This is the bottom line, and actual expectations are much higher." DeployCo's OpenAI equity holdings can also be used to acquire technology and intellectual property in the future. OpenAI executives describe the current situation as an "capability overhang" of AI: model capabilities far exceed practical applications. Chief Revenue Officer Denise Dresser stated in an internal sales email this month that the biggest bottleneck for enterprises using AI is not technology, but "deployment capability." OpenAI is also simultaneously collaborating with McKinsey and Accenture to drive enterprise adoption. Anthropic is also in talks with Blackstone and Hellman & Friedman for a similar joint venture to form an AI deployment consulting firm.

3. ByteDance Releases Next-Generation 3D Generative Model Seed3D 2.0

On April 23, ByteDance officially released a new generation of more precise 3D generative model—Seed3D 2.0. According to reports, this model uses a MoE architecture, employing sparse expert routing to expand the number of model parameters and resolution while controlling the amount of inference computation, enabling the model to generate richer texture details and more accurate metal-roughness boundaries.

... The Seed3D 2.0 technical report is now available, and the API is also live on Volcano Engine.

4. OpenAI Codex Team Fixes OpenClaw Authentication Vulnerability

On April 23, the OpenAI Codex team focused on optimizing the experience of OpenAI models within OpenClaw. Tibo Sottiaux, Codex Engineering Lead, stated that the team is collaborating with OpenClaw creator Peter Steinberger and anticipates further progress next week. Nik Pash, Codex Product Lead, discovered a critical issue during debugging: when configuring OpenClaw to use the Codex harness with an OpenAI model, the authentication process malfunctioned, causing the system to silently revert to the Pi harness, leading users to believe the Codex harness was working correctly when it wasn't. Pash submitted two pull requests, one fixing the authentication bridging and the other preventing the silent revert. The agent behavior before and after the fix was significantly different.

... When using the Pi harness, the agent only performs shallow polling with each heartbeat: reads the heartbeat file, checks Discord, returns HEARTBEAT_OK, ignores other instructions, and sometimes infers the operation to be performed but does not issue a tool call. After switching to the Codex harness, the agent enters a complete workflow: reads the workspace context, parses the task list, checks the repository, performs editing, and attempts verification. Subsequent heartbeats can connect to the progress rather than repeating the work. Pash stated that the agent's prompts and upper-level workflow logic have not been changed; the improvement comes entirely from the switching of the underlying runtime adapter (harness), that is, the way OpenClaw communicates with the model API has changed.

5. OpenAI Releases Images 2, Receives First Place in Arena.ai Evaluation

April 22nd news: This morning, OpenAI CEO Altman led a team in a 20-minute online live stream, during which he mentioned that Images 2 is the company's most powerful image generation model to date.

From the benchmark perspective, Images 2 indeed performed exceptionally well. Arena.ai, an overseas large-scale model evaluation agency, posted that OpenAI's GPT-Image-2 has topped all Image Arena leaderboards and leads Google's Nano-banana-2 in the text-based image category by a record-breaking 242 points.

DeFi Hot Topics

1. BitMine stakes another 75,600 ETH, bringing its total staked ETH to 3.471 million ETH

On April 23, according to on-chain analyst Yu Jin, after adding 100,000 ETH at noon today, BitMine just deposited 75,600 ETH ($176 million) into Ethereum PoS staking.

Currently, BitMine has staked a total of 3.471 million ETH, accounting for 70% of their total ETH holdings (4.976 million ETH).

2. Kelp DAO Releases Community Update: Actively Advancing a Solution to the rsETH Incident, Focusing on Protecting User Interests

On April 23, Kelp DAO tweeted that with the support of partners, allies, and the community over the past few days, the team is working diligently to resolve the incident, and discussions are moving in the right direction. Users always come first, and this principle will guide all subsequent actions. The team is currently working closely with all parties and has made substantial progress on several avenues, including the Arbitrum Security Committee freezing the stolen funds and Security Alliance SEAL 911 participating in the preliminary investigation. All the team's attention and efforts are focused on protecting users and strengthening the protocol.

...

3. Cardano's Parent Company Cuts Community Funding Requests to $46.8 Million for Network Scaling and Bitcoin DeFi Development

On April 23, Input Output (Cardano's parent company) reduced its 2026 funding request from the community treasury to $46.8 million, roughly half of last year's $97.5 million request. This move comes as the company begins to gradually reduce its reliance on community funding. The nine proposals the company is currently seeking support for primarily revolve around a major consensus upgrade called Leios. This upgrade is said to increase Cardano's throughput to over 1,000 transactions per second. It also includes Pogun, a system designed to introduce Bitcoin-based decentralized finance (DeFi) to the network.

The voting by approximately 1,000 elected delegates will continue until May 24th, testing whether Cardano's expanded governance mechanism, after introducing alternative development and financing structures, will treat Input Output the same way it treats other grant applicants.

4. Vitalik comments on the Polymarket hair dryer cheating case: At least three independent sources should be used

On April 23rd, Vitalik commented on the hair dryer cheating case on Polymarket, stating that for such cases, it should essentially be mandatory to use the median from at least three (or even more) independent sources. Previously, it was reported that a user used a hair dryer to artificially alter data from a Paris weather terminal, thereby manipulating the outcome and profiting $34,000.

...

5.Jupiter Launches Prediction Market Telegram Bot, Adds Clans Team-Up Feature

On April 23, according to official news, Jupiter launched the Jupiter Prediction Bot, integrating its prediction market into Telegram and adding the "Clans" feature, allowing traders to team up, discuss the market, and engage in social competition.
